chunk_summary: Micronutrient biomarkers have limitations in individual pediatric diagnosis due to variability, acute phase effects, and poor diagnostic specificity. Clinical context and response-to-therapy checkpoints are critical for cost-effective and accurate care.
learning_objectives: Understand the limitations of micronutrient biomarkers in individual pediatric diagnosis.
Learn how acute phase effects (e.g., inflammation) impact biomarker interpretation.
Identify when to use clinical context and response-to-therapy checkpoints for treatment decisions.
Recognize the role of population-level biomarkers versus individual diagnostic tools.
key_facts: Ferritin levels rise during inflammation, potentially masking iron deficiency.
Serum zinc levels are affected by diurnal variation and fasting status; a single low value rarely justifies long-term therapy.
Vitamin D testing (25(OH)D) should only be used when it changes treatment intensity; population-level low values are common.
Urinary iodine concentration is for population monitoring, not individual diagnosis.
Clinical signs like xerophthalmia or severe anemia warrant immediate treatment without waiting for biomarker results.
Response-to-therapy checkpoints, such as Hb rise after iron therapy, are often the most reliable diagnostic tools.
reference_quote: Ferritin concentrations for the assessment of iron status and iron deficiency — https://www.who.int/tools/elena/interventions/ferritin-concentrations...
document_key_topics: Limitations of micronutrient biomarkers in children
Impact of acute phase effects on biomarker accuracy
Role of clinical context in interpreting test results
Use of response-to-therapy as a diagnostic tool
